title: Deserializing objects without performing data validation is security-sensitive
description: Creating Serializable objects without data validation checks is security-sensitiveAnalyze your codeintentionality-completesecuritySecurity HotspotcweWhen an object is created via deserialization, its constructor is often not executed: the object is instead built directly from its serialized data.This means an attacker can create a malicious object and completely bypass security checks.Ask Yourself WhetherConstructors of thisSerializableclass lack relevant security checks.Security checks performed during deserialization differ from those in the constructor.There is a risk if you answered yes to any of those questions.Recommended Secure Coding PracticesEnsure validation is applied to all entry points of an application.Ensure that validation is identical regardless of how an object gets creat
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

logic.detection_conditions: Serializable classes with constructor validation but no deserialization validation
logic.triggering_conditions: Has [Serializable] AND has constructor validation (if/throw) AND missing IDeserializationCallback/OnDeserialization/property validation
logic.exclusion_conditions: Exclude test classes, classes without constructor validation, and classes with property setters that validate
never_trigger_on: Classes with validation in property setters
logic.reasoning: When objects are deserialized, constructors are bypassed. Classes that validate input in constructors must also validate during deserialization to prevent attackers from bypassing security checks. We detect Serializable classes with if/throw validation patterns in constructors, then check for IDeserializationCallback implementation, OnDeserialization method, or property setter validation.
